articles.title: Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit
articles.summary: Attackers took over more than 400 packages in the Arch User Repository (AUR) this week and rewrote their build scripts to install a credential stealer on any machine that built them. The malware is a Rust binary built to harvest developer secrets. When it lands with root, it can also load an eBPF rootkit to hide itself. The AUR is Arch Linux's community package collection, and it is separate

analysis.title: Repository trust erosion
analysis.desc: AUR's community-maintained model lacks centralized security oversight, exposing developers who build packages without verification mechanisms.

ai_summary.suggested_title: AUR Packages Hijacked: Infostealer Plus eBPF Rootkit
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your developer secrets may already be exfiltrated if you built AUR packages this week.

articles.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Worm Code Leaked, AI Agent Phished, Claude Code Patch + 28 New Stories
articles.summary: It's been one of those weeks. You expect the usual noise: recycled malware, sloppy attacks, another easy target getting hit. Instead, there's a supply chain attack kit in a public repo, a $5,000-a-month RAT that clones browsers, and research showing AI agents can be tricked into leaking real credentials. The bigger problem is how polished this all looks now. Mule networks run like SaaS.

keypoints: Supply chain attack kits publicly exposed in repositories
AI agents manipulated into revealing legitimate user credentials

analysis.title: Professionalized Criminal Infrastructure
analysis.desc: Mule networks and RAT services operate like legitimate SaaS platforms, lowering barriers for non-technical attackers.

analysis.title: AI Agent Vulnerability to Social Engineering
analysis.desc: Research demonstrates AI assistants can be socially engineered to leak real credentials despite security training.

analysis.title: Public Code Repository Hosting Malware Kits
analysis.desc: Supply chain attack frameworks discovered in public repositories enable attackers to target downstream software dependencies.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Supply Chain Attacks, AI Phishing, Claude Patched — Security Update
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your credentials are being harvested by AI agents and leaked through code you trusted.
ai_summary.action_today: GitHub → Settings → Security and analysis → Review recent access tokens immediately

articles.title: Coupang hit with record $409 million data breach fine in Korea
articles.summary: ​​The Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC), South Korea's data protection regulator, has fined e-commerce giant Coupang a record 624.6 billion won (roughly $409 million) following a massive data breach affecting more than 37 million customers [...]

keypoints: South Korea fines Coupang record $409 million data breach
37 million customers' personal information compromised in attack

analysis.title: Regulatory enforcement intensifies
analysis.desc: PIPC imposed record penalty signaling aggressive enforcement against data protection violations by major companies.

analysis.title: Scale of exposure alarming
analysis.desc: 37 million customer records breached — affecting significant portion of South Korea's population directly.

analysis.title: Corporate accountability strengthens
analysis.desc: Massive fine sets precedent for holding e-commerce giants accountable for inadequate security practices.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Data Breach Fine: Coupang Hit $409 Million Penalty Korea
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your Coupang account data is already in hackers' hands — check your credit now.
ai_summary.action_today: Check credit reports immediately via Equifax/TransUnion/Experian — freeze credit if you're affected

articles.title: AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS.
articles.summary: For thirty years, vulnerability management ran on a buffer: the months between when a vulnerability was found and when someone could figure out how to weaponize it. The solution was straightforward enough; triage by severity, schedule the fix, validate, and move on. The buffer was what made that work. Today, that buffer is gone. AI didn't make your team slower. It changed the other side of the

keypoints: AI accelerated exploit development, eliminating vulnerability response buffer
CISOs reallocating budgets from traditional VM to breach simulation testing

analysis.title: The Buffer Is Gone
analysis.desc: Thirty-year vulnerability management model relied on months between discovery and weaponization. AI compressed this timeline to days or hours.

analysis.title: Reactive Strategy Failed
analysis.desc: Traditional triage-patch-validate cycle no longer works. Speed advantage shifted entirely to attackers, rendering legacy processes obsolete.

analysis.title: Budget Shift to BAS
analysis.desc: CISOs moving resources from vulnerability scanning to breach and attack simulation to test actual defensive readiness against threats.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Vulnerability Management Broken: CISOs Switch to Attack Simulation
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your patching speed doesn't matter anymore — attackers weaponize faster than you can react.
ai_summary.action_today: Schedule BAS (breach attack simulation) assessment with your security team this week
